slides.title: Leaving a Mark ✍️
slides.content:
To write data to a file, we use `fopen` but change the mode from `"r"` (Read) to **`"w"` (Write)** or **`"a"` (Append)**.

- `"w"` Mode: Opens a file for writing. If it already has text inside, it completely nukes the old text and starts fresh. If the file doesn't exist, it creates it instantly.
- `"a"` Mode: Opens the file and safely adds new text to the very bottom, preserving the old data.

slides.title: Writing Data
slides.content: Writing is almost identical to standard `printf`. In fact, the function we use is called `fprintf` (File Print Format).

quizData.question: What happens if you open an existing 10-page text document in `"w"` (Write) mode?

options.text: The computer throws an error stating the file already exists.
It finds the bottom of page 10 and prepares to add text there safely.
It instantly deletes all 10 pages, leaving a completely blank 0-byte file ready for fresh text.

quizData.explanation: Write mode is extremely violent. It truncates (destroys) existing files. If you want to keep the data, use `"a"` (Append) mode.

slides.title: The Circle is Complete 🌌
slides.content:
You've navigated the chaotic realm of Memory and File Systems!

Between variables, arrays, structures, pointers, heap memory (`malloc`), and the hard drive (`FILE`), you now possess the core arsenal needed to conquer C.

quizData.question: Using `fprintf()` allows you to write perfectly structured strings and variables directly to a text file just as easily as writing to the terminal console.
quizData.explanation: `fprintf` uses the exact same format specifiers (`%d`, `%s`) as standard `printf`.
